ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general ricotta cheese entry (FOODON_03301710); no separate reduced-fat variant code exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers ricotta generically; no reduced-fat sub-code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for reduced-fat ricotta cheese without risk of fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of reduced-fat ricotta is approximately 60 g (about 3 heaped tablespoons), commonly used as a spread, filling, or topping.

nutrient_source.title: Ricotta cheese, part skim milk — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173420)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are taken from the Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for ricotta cheese, reduced fat (cr_id F01010022, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat is cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173420 (Ricotta, part skim milk), which is the closest approved-source equivalent for reduced-fat ricotta. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient values not reported by HPB are sourced from USDA FDC ID 173420 where available, or set to -1 where not reported.

gi_source.title: Milk, dairy products and alternatives — GI values from Atkinson et al. (2008) and University of Sydney GI Database; dairy foods with low carbohydrate content. Analogous estimate: full-fat dairy products including fresh cheeses typically GI 25–30.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for reduced-fat ricotta cheese is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. An analogous estimate of GI 27 is applied based on the published GI range for fresh dairy foods with low carbohydrate content (GI 25–30), consistent with full-fat ricotta and similar fresh cheeses as documented in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) 'International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008', Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (27 × 3.08) / 100 = 0.83, rounded to 0.8. Carbohydrate per 60 g serving = 5.14 × 0.6 = 3.08 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (99 mg/100 g), good potassium (125 mg/100 g), and high calcium (272 mg/100 g) with saturated fat (~4.85 g/100 g) below the 5 g threshold
hypertension.guidance: Reduced-fat ricotta is suitable for hypertension management due to its low sodium content, favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio, and high calcium supporting vascular tone; choose plain, unsalted varieties and verify sodium on packaging as brand formulations vary.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low sugar (0.31 g/100 g), low carbohydrate (5.14 g/100 g), low GI (~27), and high protein (11.39 g/100 g) slowing gastric emptying and attenuating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Reduced-fat ricotta is well-suited for Type 2 diabetes management; its high protein, low carbohydrate, and low GI profile support glycaemic stability, and it can be used as a protein-rich snack or meal component — choose plain varieties without added fruit, honey, or sweeteners.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.85 g/100 g) is below the 5 g/100 g threshold, fat content is reduced compared to full-fat ricotta, and the food provides calcium and protein without excessive sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Reduced-fat ricotta is a heart-friendly dairy option with saturated fat below 5 g/100 g; it can be incorporated into a cardioprotective diet as a lower-fat alternative to full-fat cheeses, while avoiding flavoured or sweetened varieties that may add sugar or sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High calcium (272 mg/100 g) critical for bone protection in RA patients on corticosteroids; saturated fat (~4.85 g/100 g) just below the 5 g/100 g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Reduced-fat ricotta is a beneficial dairy choice for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its high calcium content supporting bone density; pair with adequate vitamin D sources to maximise calcium utilisation and combine with anti-inflammatory foods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Reduced-fat ricotta cheese is a fresh Italian whey cheese made by re-cooking (ricotta means 'recooked' in Italian) the whey produced during the coagulation of low-fat or skim milk, followed by further separation and draining. The result is a soft, creamy, slightly grainy white cheese with a mild, lightly sweet, and subtly tangy flavour. Compared to full-fat ricotta, the reduced-fat variety has a slightly firmer texture and lower fat content (approximately 7.9 g/100 g versus ~13 g/100 g for full-fat). It is minimally processed and contains no artificial additives in its plain form. Nutritionally, reduced-fat ricotta is a high-protein (11.4 g/100 g), moderate-energy (138 kcal/100 g) dairy food with excellent calcium content (272 mg/100 g), low sodium (99 mg/100 g), and very low sugar (0.31 g/100 g). Saturated fat is approximately 4.85 g/100 g, below the 5 g threshold relevant to cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis dietary guidelines. It has a low estimated glycemic index (~27) and a glycemic load of approximately 0.8 per 60 g serving, making it suitable for Type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis management. Clinically, its high protein content slows gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial glucose response, making it an excellent snack or meal component for blood glucose stability. Its calcium content is particularly valuable for patients on corticosteroids who are at risk of bone loss. Reduced-fat ricotta is widely used in Italian and Western cuisine as a filling for pasta (lasagne, cannelloni, ravioli), in desserts (cheesecake, cannoli), as a spread on toast, or as a topping for fruit and salads. It is gluten-free and suitable for vegetarians. The primary allergen is milk.

confidence_reason: Primary macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and micronutrient values are cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173420 (Ricotta, part skim milk), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, consistent with published dairy GI ranges.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per Singapore HPB Foodo database (F01010022) and consistent with USDA FDC ID 173420 which reports 0 g dietary fibre for part-skim ricotta. This is expected as ricotta is a dairy product with no plant-derived fibre. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FDC ID 173420 for part-skim ricotta. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_e_mg set to -1: these values are not reported in the Singapore HPB Foodo database for this entry, and vitamin D and E data are not consistently reported for this food type in the USDA SR Legacy dataset for part-skim ricotta. Set to -1 to indicate data not available from approved sources for this specific reduced-fat variant. 4. Saturated fat value of 4.85 g/100 g is derived from USDA FDC ID 173420 (part-skim ricotta) as the closest approved-source equivalent; the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 5. Glycemic index is set to 27 (analogous estimate, low GI category) based on published GI ranges for fresh dairy foods (GI 25-30) per Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283, as no direct GI value for ricotta cheese exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 60 g serving = (27 × 3.08) / 100 = 0.8. 6. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are the closest defensible ontology anchors for ricotta cheese; no reduced-fat specific codes exist in either system as of April 2026.
